1030	## 9. Concurrency, Thread-Safety, Edge Cases & Extensibility
1031	
1032	**DEFAULT: describe verbally. Write code only if asked.**
1033	
1034	🗣️ **Step 1 — Name the exact race:** "The race is in BOTH `get` and `put`, and that's the subtle part most people miss. `put` on a full cache does a check-then-act: it checks `len >= capacity`, then evicts and inserts. Two threads can both pass the check and both insert — over capacity, or both evict the same victim and corrupt the linked list. And `get` isn't safe either: it calls `_bump`, which splices a node between two buckets — two concurrent splices on the same bucket tangle the pointers."
1035	
1036	🗣️ **Step 2 — State the solution:** "I add ONE lock to the cache and wrap the entire `get`/`put` body in it. Not per-key striping — because a single `put` touches the victim key, the new key, two buckets, AND the shared min-freq pointer. There's no single 'resource' to stripe on; the unit of atomicity is the whole structure. One lock around the whole operation is both correct and the simplest thing that works."
1037	
1038	🗣️ **Step 3 — Name the trade-off:** "The coarse lock serialises all cache operations — only one get/put at a time. For a single-process cache that's usually fine because each op is microseconds. If it became a bottleneck, the real answer isn't finer locks (the min-freq pointer is global shared state) — it's SHARDING: split the keyspace into N independent caches, each with its own lock, so different keys don't contend."
1039	
1040	🗣️ **Step 4 — Distributed case (if asked):** "Across multiple processes/servers, an in-process lock protects nothing. You'd use a shared store like Redis, which is single-threaded per shard so it serialises commands for you, and lean on its native data structures — or push the policy to the store. At that point 'exact LFU' is often relaxed to APPROXIMATE LFU (e.g. Redis's `allkeys-lfu` with a probabilistic counter) because perfect global frequency ordering across shards is expensive."
1041	
1042	**CONCURRENCY VOCABULARY**
1043	
1044	| Term | Plain meaning | Where it appears here |
1045	|---|---|---|
1046	| **Race condition** | Two threads touch shared state at once; result depends on timing | two `put`s past the capacity check; two `_bump` splices on one bucket |
1047	| **Critical section** | The block that must run as one atomic step | the entire `get`/`put` body |
1048	| **Check-then-act** | Check a condition, then act — but state changed between | "saw `len < capacity`, then inserted" |
1049	| **Atomic** | All-or-nothing; no half-done state observable | what the lock gives the get/put body |
1050	| **Mutex / lock** | A token only one thread holds at a time | `threading.RLock` on the cache |
1051	| **Reentrant (RLock)** | Same thread can re-acquire a lock it holds | safe default since the guarded body calls guarded-style helpers |
1052	| **Lock granularity** | How much one lock covers — coarse vs fine | we chose ONE coarse lock; the min-freq pointer is global, so finer locks don't help |
1053	| **Pessimistic locking** | Grab the lock before touching state | the in-process `RLock` |
1054	| **Optimistic locking** | Don't lock; write conditionally, retry on conflict | the distributed/DB compare-and-set |
1055	| **Sharding** | Split the keyspace into independent pieces | the real throughput fix — N caches, N locks |
1056	| **Deadlock** | Two threads each hold a lock the other needs | avoided: we only ever hold ONE lock |
1057	| **Starvation** | A thread never gets the lock | not a risk; the lock is held microseconds |
1058	
1059	**CONCURRENCY FOLLOW-UP Q&A**
1060	
1061	> ❓ "Why one coarse lock instead of a lock per key?"
1062	> "Because a single `put` mutates more than one key's worth of state — it evicts a DIFFERENT key, moves nodes between two buckets, and updates the global min-freq pointer. Per-key locks would have to be acquired for the new key AND the victim AND coordinate the shared pointer — that's lock-ordering complexity and deadlock risk for no real win, since the pointer serialises everything anyway. One lock around the whole op is correct and simple."
1063	
1064	> ❓ "Why `RLock` and not a plain `Lock`?"
1065	> "Defensive reentrancy. The guarded body calls helpers (`_bump`, `_evict_one`) and I want to be free to have a helper take the lock too without deadlocking the same thread. An `RLock` lets the owning thread re-enter and only frees on the outermost exit. Here it's a safe default; with a plain Lock I'd have to guarantee no helper ever re-acquires."
1066	
1067	> ❓ "What's the deadlock risk?"
1068	> "None. Deadlock needs two locks taken in different orders. I hold exactly one lock at a time, and there's no I/O or callback inside it, so there's no ordering cycle."
1069	
1070	> ❓ "Pessimistic or optimistic — which and why?"
1071	> "Pessimistic in-process: the critical section is microseconds and the structure is shared global state, so a short-held lock with no retry loop is simplest. For the DISTRIBUTED case I flip to optimistic — a Redis/DB compare-and-set — because you can't hold a Python lock across servers."
1072	
1073	> ❓ "It's read-heavy — would a read-write lock help?"
1074	> "Not here, because `get` is NOT a pure read — it mutates by bumping the frequency. A read-write lock only helps when readers don't write. Every `get` writes, so there's no read-only path to parallelise. I'd reach for it only if I added a genuinely non-mutating query like 'peek without bumping' under heavy load."
1075	
1076	❓ **IF ASKED — the thread-safe version:**
1077	
1078	**Part A — the no-op hook + subclass:**
1079	```python copy
1080	# ─── §6 core: the hook does NOTHING ───────────────────────────────────────
1081	@contextmanager
1082	def _lock(self) -> Iterator[None]:
1083	    yield
1084	
1085	# ─── §9 thread-safe subclass: overrides ONLY the hook ──────────────────────
1086	class ThreadSafeLFUCache(LFUCache):
1087	    def __init__(self, capacity: int) -> None:
1088	        super().__init__(capacity)
1089	        self._mutex = threading.RLock()   # RLock: reentrant, safe default
1090	
1091	    @contextmanager
1092	    def _lock(self) -> Iterator[None]:
1093	        with self._mutex:                 # ONE lock guards the whole structure
1094	            yield
1095	```
